result:
The Quiet Edge  

There was a moment, sometime around late October, when the air felt dialed back to its purest frequency—thin, metallic, almost embarrassed to make noise. Dusk had gathered over the complex of brick buildings and roof gardens where I live, and across a sprawl of satellite dishes and chimney pots I watched a small brown bat stitch sharp corners against a sky the color of oxidized copper. I was up on the eighth-floor catwalk, elbows on the railing, notebook open in one hand, pen lost again somewhere between pockets. In the distance, the river traded a last flash of peach-colored light before sinking into itself. There was that specific kind of cold—buried under the day’s heat yet unmistakably present—that waits on corners, then slips inside your sleeves without asking.  

I stayed put, letting the evening rearrange me. I like those transitional minutes when the world forgets to administrate itself, and you discover you’re breathing the same air shared by ghosts, children, pigeons, night-shift cabbies, and expiring leaves alike. That is the minute I kept trying to describe to friends later, and failing. I said things like: “The horizon went soft at the edges,” or “The clouds had the texture of old velvet given up on glamour.” When they asked why I’d been on the roof at all, I mumbled about checking the water tower for leaks, but that was only the cover story.  

The truth is, I’d climbed the exterior stair because I had misplaced the boundary between what could be known and what could only be guessed, and I needed elevation to see if it might reappear in panorama. Nothing was deeply wrong, or even especially upended, yet I carried a hollow-grapefruit sensation in my chest—sour sweetness, thin membranes of anxiety, small resistant seeds—ever since a phone call the night before. My sister, from another timezone, had told me the antiques shop our grandmother once ran in a shoestring strip mall on the outskirts of Atlanta was scheduled to be bulldozed: “Progress, whatever that means,” she sighed. The building had been empty for years, colonized by squirrels and brittle catalog receipts, but it existed in my head like an unstruckt word, keeping my childhood in a trusted file. Now the demolition company wanted family permission to cart away whatever rot had not been auctioned. My sister had run into the county clerk at the post office, he’d mentioned it, she was asking me what we ought to do. And the truth? Neither of us had a clue.  

I could picture the front window like a photograph developed in dream-chemistry: the inverted reflection of highway traffic sliding across dusty glass jars, behind them stacks of setter-figurines and carved coconut monkeys grinning in sunglasses of kudzu shade. A purple post-war Kelvinator refrigerator hummed in the recesses like a monk. My grandmother—whom we called only Nene—had run her pan-sharpened nails down the chipping Wedgewood painted door and said to six-year-old me, “Objects wait for us. We decide whether we still want them.” At six, I took objects at their word. Now I was learning they waited somewhere else entirely, to which none of us held the address.  

I remembered that spacious rooftop for a whole week afterward. My therapist clocked it immediately as a “classic hermit phase” and suggested I should consider group pottery, because working clay in pairs is hardwired to produce oxytocin. I nodded politely, visualized symmetrical ascots of beige clay looped around the wrists of strangers. Instead I woke at dawn and rode buses-free buses to the marina, walking the slippery pier planks with a thermos of burnt-smelling office coffee. During those walks I began naming every small, flying thing I saw: least sandpiper, horned grebe, winter wren, and “unidentified dark blur,” which sounded like a good punk band. I scribbled them on ticket stubs from my coat pocket, then lost the stubs. The year was folding into itself and time was accelerating the way it does when you’ve misplaced something you can’t name.  

On a Tuesday, November wrung cold water across the city early, and I found myself bicycling to the abandoned lot where the old civic ballroom stood—an art-deco affair painted migraine pink. Its doors had been chained since the nineties, but a plywood section at the back had rotted loose, perfect for ducking through. Inside, the grand plaster shell held a dimness so thick it buzzed. Splints of colored glass clung to tall lancet windows; chandeliers swagged like condemned hawks. A seagull launched itself from the gallery rail and circled once before exiting through a punched skylight.  

My footfalls ricocheted. I spoke into the hollow to test the echo—“Hello, it’s me”—and the me came back elder and slower, threaded with plaster dust. I thought of Nene’s antiques shop again, and of how buildings outlive their original bodies just the same as people. It suddenly seemed cowardly to let strangers tear her place down without at least visiting, one last inventory of ghosts and unclaimed brooches. Within minutes, still standing anonymous in that peeling ballroom, I decided to drive to Atlanta.  

The decision was spur-of-the-moment, but it slit open a day-mare of logistics: winter storm watch across two mountain ranges, holiday traffic like blood cells gone tangled, and my own anemic bank account. Worse, the bulldozers were slated for Friday— forty-eight hours away. My apartment rent was overdue; I had client revisions on three separate deadlines. Despite the manic impracticality, I could feel the gravity of Southern humidity, the frog-croaked creek behind Nene’s shop, calling me with a low cello note vibrating somewhere between memory and cartilage.  

I threw together a duffel of weather-mismatched clothes, laptop, trail mix, an old cassette of Sam Cooke dubbed for car tape-deck emergencies. Before dawn Wednesday I took the interstate under a moon lean as a fingernail clipping. The night smelled of gasoline frosting. I wrapped Nene’s hand-knitted afghan—brown and orange zigzags straight from nineteen seventy-two—around my shoulders like a transit cape.  

By the time Memphis receded in shrinking sun, my worldview had already undergone mild dilution. Driving is hypnotic: mile-markers flutter like discarded hope, billboards shout diagnoses you didn’t know needed naming, egrets lift up out of fields in modular assembly. Every so often the radio spat scratchy orchestral concerts from state universities, making distant cultures feel strangely on-ramp accessible. I thought, This must have been how country got invented—staring at the white broken line until lonesome took the wheel.  

Outside Nashville, a tire blew. The steering wheel jagged left, heart trampoline high; I pumped brakes like first-aid instructors taught yet never meant for this velocity. On the narrow shoulder, I unfolded myself into gravel dust. Cold rain began—thin needles spitting sideways—and I wrestled the spare like a carnival strongman with stage fright. My hands went numb. Meanwhile, six lanes of commuters screamed past, stale cheerios of light and brake red. I found myself laughing uncontrollably, because twenty minutes earlier I’d been philosophizing with Sam Cooke about the cupidity of creation and now I was being water-boarded by an overhead cloud of my own absent-minded planning.  

Once back on the highway, the incident settled into my chest like broth starting to simmer. Life had shoved neon Post-it on my windshield: YOU ARE NOT SAFE. I had always known this, theoretically. But something about kneeling on the asphalt while a tempest cheese-grated my face made mortality folk-art vivid. I thought of my clients’ looming email tantrums and felt an unexpected indifference—ironic since I was driving not toward some transcendence but merely to hold an old building’s hand while it died.  

Cities slid across the windshield. Chattanooga at dusk: steel bridges married in spidery silhouette, bright chemical washes against henna hills. Then the climb up through the Blue Ridge, headlights sweeping cliff faces like prom flash photos. I crossed into Georgia past mineral quarries the color of surgical bruise. Midnight brought me, finally, onto the exit ramp that once led to Nene’s world: a four-lane now supersized, with strip of fast food emitting 24-hour halo. Memory flat-out refused to overlay the past onto the present. All the landmarks had moved.  

I parked at the shuttered Piggly-Wiggly. The rain had stopped; what moved in its place was a fog smelling of diesel and overripe kudzu. A single sodium lamp buzzed, tinting the lot amber. From there I walked the two blocks that had stretched larger in childhood, past the old barber’s converted into a vape lounge, past the carwash that played gospel Thursdays. The antique shop façade looked smaller, though scalloped tin awnings remained. The front door was padlocked; a Xeroxed notice fluttered about condemnation hearings. The windows, however— still the same dusty jars, but the monkey carvings were gone. I cupped hands against glass. Moonlight diluted as it passed through cobwebs, landing wanly on shelves of cracked carnival masks, a taxidermied owl hunched in perpetual surprise, and the Kelvinator still humming, its door wired shut like a grounded teen.  

I rested my forehead on the cool pane. Somewhere down the asphalt my younger self sat on the same sidewalk, licking a blueberry popsicle and counting horseflies. That version of me couldn’t possibly know we would still be counting decades later, in the body of a person who had failed at various liaisons, who owned four mismatched forks, who could ace HTML validation yet sometimes forgot how to cook rice. I whispered through the glass: “I kept the pact. I remembered you better than figure the rest.” A modest lie; memory had filed numerous losses under miscellaneous.  

I circled to the alley. The rear service door hung drunkenly on one hinge; someone had pried it sideways so trespassers could slip by. Inside lay a smell I can’t parse other than time: cedar shavings, dry rot, a ghost of sachet. My phone torch lit aisles jagged with toppled crates. I moved slowly, half-thief, half-child. Here were Nene’s laminations: a 1948 calendar still taped beside the fuse box, picture of roses fossilized behind yellowed varnish. My throat tightened the way it does when you see first snow unexpectedly.  

In the office alcove I found the shallow drawer where Nene kept extras—ballpoint pens printed for long-bankrupt conventions, rubber bands brittle as celery. I opened the chunky Rolodex; she never computerized anything. Cards fluttered out like moths: Mr. Livernois (wants brass spittoon), Miss Velma (will trade brooch for costume jewellery). I pictured the card for myself—Andrew (return for pocketknife repair)—though I had no memory of anything broken. I stuck one card in my pocket for ballast: Mrs Beale, turquoise kettle, owes $22. Debt paid or unpaid, the kettle now violet with oxide. Somehow possessing this fragment felt like collateral against forgetting.  

Upstairs was the small attic room accessible via a pull-down ladder. In childhood we grandchildren had been told it held “forbidden antiques,” which meant mostly boxed Christmas ornaments plus a melancholy rocking horse whose paint flaked like sunburnt skin. I climbed. The air up there was even thicker—egg-yolk light and the mildewy breath of cardboard. A plastic Santa, nose blunted, greeted me from a lawn chair princess throne. I found Nene’s steamer trunk, unlocked. Inside were yellowed newspaper ovations, theatre programs, receipts for a 1936 engagement party—her own? I hadn’t realized she’d ever been young. Tucked at bottom lay a spiral notebook. When I lifted it the spine shed flakes like confetti. The cover said Daybook May-Aug 1979.  

I sat on the dusty boards and opened it under flashlight. Nene’s handwriting surprised me by being neat, narrow-ruled like stitching. Early entries bore prices of side tables, customer quirks, local election grumbles. But midway she shifted: No buyers today, sat with widow Turner until sunset. She spoke of yellow birds like pieces of sun come loose. That image—sun crumbs—lodged under my ribs. Later: I dreamt last night of a giant clock without hands. You cannot be late or on-time to infinity, only inside it. I felt I was reading a Rosetta Stone of my inheritance: the conviction that time circled more than marched. When my father died that year (1980, I calculated), Nene wrote: He went out the way a note fades from piano strings, last shimmer steady though you know it will not return.. I closed the notebook abruptly; words too intimate. The room pressed softly around me with witness.  

I descended the ladder feeling smuggled. The shop floor creaked under me like an old confidant grasping my wrist. In the jade fluorescent spill from streetlamps, objects glimmered: prie-dieu marred by candle-scar, cracked whale-oil lamp. A single upright piano (story had it salvaged from riverboat 1938) stood lid crashed open, exposing yellowed ivories like teeth mid-laugh. I drifted over, lifted middle C. Its voice was a raspy baritone but still true. I played “Little Brown Jug” with one finger, the only song Nene tolerated. The sound traveled the shop in sepia ripples.  

Then I noticed newer water damage across the plaster ceiling. The drip had begun forming stalactite hugs of ochre. By morning whole sections could collapse. The bulldozers weren’t villainous; the place was gently folding itself. The elegance of entropy brought tears lousy with salt; I wiped my face on Nene’s afghan, which smelled of cedar gunpowder.  

I spent the night sitting on the Kelvinator’s footstep, back against corrugated tin, phone dead, city slowing to intermittent coyote howl somewhere east. At first light, birds converse in staircase chirps. I considered relocating the remaining items—what a ridiculous project. Instead I took Polaroids with an impulse camera I found under crates of mothballs. Each flash made the room a negative that lingered pink on retinas. I boxed small objects that spoke directly to me: a set wooden bird marionette, a copper flour sifter whose sieve patterns looked like outer space constellations. Loaded these into car trunk like contraband.  

Friday morning. Demo crew arrived at nine sharp: fluorescent vests, two enormous hydraulic buckets, radio thumping fem-pop anthem. Foreman approached politely, clip-boarded, offered coffee thermos; I answered only with squint. He asked “official family”? I nodded. I mentioned waiting for the friable ceiling, but they laughed gently—ceiling’s Monday’s problem. They invited me to pick final keepsake before the claw. I chose the Kelvinator door, small enamel badge reading ‘Ice Cold’. One crewmember unbolted with cordless gun. The badge fit pocket.  

They gave me ten minutes to exit. A soft percussive commentary echoed from trucks idle. I walked to the footpath outside and watched the teeth of the yellow excavator bite a first mouthful from the western wall—plaster sighing, a puff of ironic lace. Each chunk of brick toppled with loosened decades. I waited until whole façade folded like a theatre flat; dust rolled street-bound just slow enough you could see salad days dissolve. I found myself mouthing Nene’s words: “Objects wait for us.” Where would they wait now?  

Driving back north, the landscape rolled reversed. I stopped in Tennessee to sleep in the car outside a twenty-four-hour truckstop. A harvest moon rose, fat, tangerine. Across three spaces, a semi driver grilled something on a hibachi. Night insects dive-bombed the dome light. Inside the car, climate thinned to visibility: every exhale ghost of breath. I laid the Kelvinator badge on the dash. Moonlight struck it oblong, and— I swear— frost bloomed across the metal like instantaneous spider silk. I touched the lacy fringe; it melted, cold water dotting my fingertip, warm blood underneath. A small miracle, unverified.  

Continued north Saturday. Home shoreline drew near like a breath holding itself. By dusk I eased the weary hatch-back back into the city’s arterial traffic. Red cranes hovered over the river like lampries of renewal. I wasn’t returning clawed but dismantled— flat cola taste on tongue, cassette chewed, ankles shot from clutch leg cramps. Yet sound of piano baritone played beneath ribcage percussion. Sunday, I woke to first sifting snow of season, flakes as tentative as latecomers. I sat wrapped in Nene’s afghan over steam vent on my balcony. Apartment blocks glimmered pewter. Somewhere a trumpeter practiced arpeggios that punched holes in distance. The Kelvinator badge was cold as ever, though the radiator pipe blared heat. I placed it on the sill. Snowflakes landed—hesitated—melted, confessing small histories to the metal surface: how far they had fallen, through what altitude cold, what whisper of shape held by cloud mothers.  

I realise I am approaching 2000 words and need to ease toward a landing. How to do this? The story doesn’t resolve along the neat embankments we’re told to expect. There is no epiphany in golden script. I have the Daybook; I have routines that now include sitting for five minutes each morning recollecting, like meditation with dangling participles. I have calloused edges on my fingertip from the fridge badge’s frost burn, a tiny scar I’ll narrate for unconvinced bartenders. One drawer stores Afghans, another holds the wooden bird who moves his beak by dangling string. One day I may mail the turquoise kettle card to whoever Mrs Beale became— she deserves notice of settlement, however delayed. My clients fired me then rehired me; they thought my attachments quaint, then discovered that “ralslow, fnattic frail” deadlines granted me a newly engineered calm.  

Sometimes I still climb to the roof during dusk-share hours. The winter air is sharper, the river’s skin furls iron grey beneath water treatment outflow. One evening last week the small brown bat returned—same impossible cartography. I raised a silent toast with drained coffee mug. The afghan around my shoulders smells increasingly of outside smoke, car exhaust, other memories desperate to hitchhike. But I hold the line: you accommodate a finite cargo you can carry without dislocating heart.  

I do not know if this narrative convinces anyone other than myself. Still, beneath every sentence runs the humid Georgian staircase of Nene’s shop, termites gently civil in the struts, children asleep in moving-car backseats while radios sang America. I am driving again, eternally, spaced apart from myself by lost geometries of love, while overhead the mountains trap night like cupped palms over a lightning bug. One stroke of fieldstone, one scrap of lullaby, swallowed by distance. But meantime I have pinned the badge to my coat lapel. When noon strikes, the frost returns like persistent vow. I touch metal, feel cool certainty—and know at least three lines of horizon yet to discover.

I chose the Kelvinator door, small enamel badge reading ‘Ice Cold’. One crewmember unbolted with cordless gun. The badge fit pocket.  

They gave me ten minutes to exit. A soft percussive commentary echoed from trucks idle. I walked to the footpath outside and watched the teeth of the yellow excavator bite a first mouthful from the western wall—plaster sighing, a puff of ironic lace. Each chunk of brick toppled with loosened decades. I waited until whole façade folded like a theatre flat; dust rolled street-bound just slow enough you could see salad days dissolve. I found myself mouthing Nene’s words: “Objects wait for us.” Where would they wait now?  

Driving back north, the landscape rolled reversed. I stopped in Tennessee to sleep in the car outside a twenty-four-hour truckstop. A harvest moon rose, fat, tangerine. Across three spaces, a semi driver grilled something on a hibachi. Night insects dive-bombed the dome light. Inside the car, climate thinned to visibility: every exhale ghost of breath. I laid the Kelvinator badge on the dash. Moonlight struck it oblong, and— I swear— frost bloomed across the metal like instantaneous spider silk. I touched the lacy fringe; it melted, cold water dotting my fingertip, warm blood underneath. A small miracle, unverified.  

Continued north Saturday. Home shoreline drew near like a breath holding itself. By dusk I eased the weary hatch-back back into the city’s arterial traffic. Red cranes hovered over the river like lampries of renewal. I wasn’t returning clawed but dismantled— flat cola taste on tongue, cassette chewed, ankles shot from clutch leg cramps. Yet sound of piano baritone played beneath ribcage percussion. Sunday, I woke to first sifting snow of season, flakes as tentative as latecomers. I sat wrapped in Nene’s afghan over steam vent on my balcony. Apartment blocks glimmered pewter. Somewhere a trumpeter practiced arpeggios that punched holes in distance. The Kelvinator badge was cold as ever, though the radiator pipe blared heat. I placed it on the sill. Snowflakes landed—hesitated—melted, confessing small histories to the metal surface: how far they had fallen, through what altitude cold, what whisper of shape held by cloud mothers.  

I realise I am approaching 2000 words and need to ease toward a landing. How to do this? The story doesn’t resolve along the neat embankments we’re told to expect. There is no epiphany in golden script. I have the Daybook; I have routines that now include sitting for five minutes each morning recollecting, like meditation with dangling participles. I have calloused edges on my fingertip from the fridge badge’s frost burn, a tiny scar I’ll narrate for unconvinced bartenders. One drawer stores Afghans, another holds the wooden bird who moves his beak by dangling string. One day I may mail the turquoise kettle card to whoever Mrs Beale became— she deserves notice of settlement, however delayed. My clients fired me then rehired me; they thought my attachments quaint, then discovered that “ralslow, fnattic frail” deadlines granted me a newly engineered calm.  

Sometimes I still climb to the roof during dusk-share hours. The winter air is sharper, the river’s skin furls iron grey beneath water treatment outflow. One evening last week the small brown bat returned—same impossible cartography. I raised a silent toast with drained coffee mug. The afghan around my shoulders smells increasingly of outside smoke, car exhaust, other memories desperate to hitchhike. But I hold the line: you accommodate a finite cargo you can carry without dislocating heart.  

I do not know if this narrative convinces anyone other than myself. Still, beneath every sentence runs the humid Georgian staircase of Nene’s shop, termites gently civil in the struts, children asleep in moving-car backseats while radios sang America. I am driving again, eternally, spaced apart from myself by lost geometries of love, while overhead the mountains trap night like cupped palms over a lightning bug. One stroke of fieldstone, one scrap of lullaby, swallowed by distance. But meantime I have pinned the badge to my coat lapel. When noon strikes, the frost returns like persistent vow. I touch metal, feel cool certainty—and know at least three lines of horizon yet to discover.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
